skill.name: playwright-skill
skill.description: Browser automation skill for Playwright that detects local dev servers, writes temporary test scripts, runs browser checks, fills forms, captures screenshots, and validates web behavior.
skill.summary: Automate browser testing and web UI validation with Playwright.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings are mostly documentation and Playwright false positives, but the runtime intentionally executes arbitrary JavaScript from files, inline arguments, or stdin. I found no evidence of prompt injection or malicious exfiltration, but the skill has high inherent risk because it runs code, installs dependencies, reads environment configuration, writes temporary files, and can browse arbitrary sites.

high_findings.title: Arbitrary JavaScript Execution Through Universal Runner
high_findings.description: The runner accepts code from a path, inline command argument, or stdin, writes it into a temporary JavaScript file, and loads it with require(). This is intentional for automation, but it gives any supplied script full Node.js privileges in the skill directory.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code path directly reads caller-supplied JavaScript and executes it with require(). The behavior is explicit and does not sandbox filesystem, process, or network access.

medium_findings.title: Automatic Dependency Installation Uses External Commands
medium_findings.description: If Playwright is missing, the runner executes npm install and npx playwright install chromium. Commands are hardcoded, so command injection is not evident, but execution can reach the network and run package installation logic from a community skill.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: execSync calls and npm scripts are directly present. The risk is supply-chain and execution scope, not user-controlled shell injection.

medium_findings.title: Environment Header Injection Can Propagate Sensitive Values
medium_findings.description: The helper reads PW_HEADER_NAME, PW_HEADER_VALUE, and PW_EXTRA_HEADERS, then merges those values into browser context headers. Documentation suggests adding authentication tokens globally, which can expose secrets if users browse untrusted external targets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variables are clearly read and attached to browser requests. Exposure depends on user-selected target URLs, so this is a contextual data-handling risk.

medium_findings.title: Localhost Dev Server Probing
medium_findings.description: The helper sends HEAD requests to common localhost ports to discover development servers. The probe is limited to localhost, but it is still network reconnaissance behavior that should be disclosed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly iterates over common ports and sends localhost HTTP requests. The limited hostname reduces severity compared with external scanning.

low_findings.title: Playwright DOM Evaluation Misidentified As Dynamic Code Eval
low_findings.description: The static eval finding at lib/helpers.js uses page.$$eval to extract text from browser DOM elements. This is a normal Playwright API call, not Node.js eval on arbitrary strings.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The API call is page.$$eval with a selector and browser-side callback. It does not evaluate a constructed JavaScript string in Node.js.

low_findings.title: Documentation Examples Trigger Pattern False Positives
low_findings.description: Many external command, weak crypto, hidden file, and hardcoded URL detections are markdown examples or Playwright reference snippets. I did not find evidence that these examples are malicious instructions or prompt injection attempts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are fenced setup commands, localhost examples, keyboard shortcuts, or configuration snippets. They explain normal Playwright usage rather than hidden execution logic.

dangerous_patterns.title: Runtime Loading Of Generated Temporary Script
dangerous_patterns.description: The runner writes supplied automation code to a .temp-execution file and requires it. This pattern is equivalent to executing arbitrary local JavaScript with Node.js privileges.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The temporary file path is generated by the runner and then passed directly to require(). The executed content comes from caller-provided code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package Installation From Runner
dangerous_patterns.description: The runner can invoke npm install and Playwright browser installation when dependencies are missing. This introduces network and package lifecycle execution during skill use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are hardcoded and visible, which reduces injection risk. The operational risk remains because installation commands execute external tooling.

content.value_statement: Manual browser checks slow down web development and miss repeatable issues. This skill creates and runs Playwright automation for screenshots, forms, links, login flows, and responsive UI checks.
actual_capabilities: Detects running localhost development servers on common ports before testing.
Generates Playwright scripts for page visits, form filling, clicks, and assertions.
Runs automation through a Node.js Playwright wrapper from files, inline code, or stdin.
Captures screenshots for desktop, tablet, and mobile viewport checks.
Provides helpers for safe clicks, safe typing, cookie banners, table extraction, and retries.
Supports custom HTTP headers through environment variables for controlled test traffic.
limitations: Requires Node.js and Playwright browser dependencies to be installed or installable.
Executes generated JavaScript with local Node.js privileges, so scripts need review.
Dev server detection only checks localhost ports and may miss custom network setups.
Browser automation can be fragile when pages use unstable selectors or heavy animation.

use_cases.title: Validate a Local Web App
use_cases.description: Detect the running dev server, open key pages, check visible content, and save screenshots for review.

use_cases.title: Exercise Login and Form Flows
use_cases.description: Automate login, form entry, redirects, and success messages with repeatable Playwright scripts.

use_cases.title: Review Responsive Layouts
use_cases.description: Capture desktop, tablet, and mobile views to compare layout behavior across screen sizes.

prompt_templates.title: Check a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Playwright skill to find my local dev server, open the home page, report the title, and take a full-page screenshot.

prompt_templates.title: Test a Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Playwright to test the contact form. Fill each field with test data, submit it, and confirm the success or error message.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Viewports
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a responsive check for this URL across desktop, tablet, and mobile sizes. Save screenshots and summarize layout issues.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a User Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Playwright script for the signup-to-dashboard flow. Parameterize the target URL, use stable selectors, capture screenshots at each step, and report failures.

output_examples.input: Test the marketing page on my local dev server.
output: Detected one local server on port 3001.
Opened the marketing page and captured desktop and mobile screenshots.
Reported visible layout issues, console errors, and page title results.

output_examples.input: Check if login redirects to the dashboard.
output: Opened the login page, filled the requested credentials, and submitted the form.
Verified whether the browser reached the dashboard route.
Returned a concise pass or fail result with the observed URL.

output_examples.input: Find broken links on the docs page.
output: Collected links from the page and checked their responses.
Separated working links from failed or unreachable links.
Summarized the failures without exposing raw automation code.

best_practices: Use local or trusted target URLs unless the automation script is carefully reviewed.
Parameterize URLs and credentials instead of hardcoding sensitive values in scripts.
Prefer stable selectors such as data-testid attributes for repeatable tests.
anti_patterns: Do not run untrusted inline JavaScript through the universal runner.
Do not send global authentication headers to unknown external sites.
Do not rely on fixed waits when Playwright selectors or load-state waits are available.

faq.question: What does this skill automate?
faq.answer: It automates browser tasks with Playwright, including page checks, forms, screenshots, links, and responsive layouts.

faq.question: Does it need a running web server?
faq.answer: Local testing works best with a running dev server. The skill can detect common localhost ports.

faq.question: Can it test external websites?
faq.answer: Yes, but use trusted targets and review any headers or credentials before browsing external sites.

faq.question: Why is the security risk high?
faq.answer: The runner executes supplied JavaScript with Node.js privileges and can install dependencies when Playwright is missing.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Where are generated test scripts stored?
faq.answer: The skill instructions direct generated scripts to /tmp so project files are not cluttered.
